title: Alabama Offers Three Tricks to Fix Poor Urban Schools
content:
Just six years ago, Democratic states seemed better at educating children, as their pupils scored higher on reading and math tests. But since the pandemic, students in places like Alabama, Florida, and Texas have shot up the national rankings. This “southern surge” is even more impressive when adjusted for student poverty. Red states have gone back to basics, enacting new rules that require teaching reading via phonics and holding failing schools accountable. These decisions matter, but school districts also run small experiments to figure out what works.

In Birmingham, Alabama, a few clever policies seem to be turning things around in a district where most qualify for subsidized lunches. The city went from having fifteen failing schools to just one in the past two years. Their first major success tackled absenteeism by offering housing lotteries to parents whose children maintained perfect attendance. Prosecutors also began helping truant students meet basic needs instead of arresting them. As a result, the share of students who were chronically absent halved in four years.

Next, the district successfully prevented holiday learning loss by introducing voluntary 'intersession' classes over breaks. To remove barriers, buses still ran and lunches were served, leading half of the district's pupils to enroll by summer. The city now pays local college students and retired teachers to tutor those falling behind. These efforts drastically boosted grade-level reading rates and high school graduation rates. The mayor also launched a program paying full college tuition for local graduates.

Some economists remain skeptical, arguing that modest local policy changes are unlikely to explain the entire academic bump. They believe bigger state policies like phonics are the primary driver, and local tricks might not easily replicate elsewhere. However, optimists note that big, evidence-based schemes usually start as small-town experiments. Leaders believe that places like Birmingham are proving that serving disadvantaged children effectively is entirely possible.
